stories.headline: Red Flag Warning Issued for Denver Metro Wednesday as Fire Danger Remains Critical
stories.summary:
The National Weather Service in Boulder issued a Red Flag Warning for the Denver metro area Wednesday, in effect from noon to 8 p.m., citing high temperatures, gusty winds and critically dry conditions. The warning covers the Front Range corridor between Loveland and Castle Rock and extends east across most of the Eastern Plains. Relative humidity dropped to around 11 percent, with wind gusts up to 29 mph in Denver and 30-40 mph along the Wyoming border.

Denver temperatures were expected to reach 88 degrees Wednesday, about 6 degrees above normal for mid-June. Meteorologists warned that conditions are favorable for rapid fire spread, particularly in areas that have not received significant recent precipitation. Residents were advised to follow burn ban restrictions, avoid driving on dry grass and properly discard cigarettes.

A cold front arriving Wednesday night was expected to bring relief, dropping overnight temperatures to around 53 degrees. Highs will moderate to the upper 70s on Thursday before hot, sunny and dry conditions return Friday, with temperatures climbing back into the lower 90s. Scattered storms are possible over the weekend as another cold front approaches.

stories.headline: Fast-Moving Fire Destroys Apartment Building in North Rifle, Prompts Evacuations
stories.summary: A fast-moving fire broke out in north Rifle on Wednesday, destroying an apartment building and prompting mandatory evacuation orders, according to Colorado River Fire Rescue. The blaze began as a structure fire before spreading into surrounding wildland, consistent with the elevated fire danger gripping much of Western Colorado. First responders were on scene as firefighting efforts continued.

stories.headline: San Miguel County Deploys 21,000-Gallon Water Tanks to Bolster Wildfire Response Amid Historic Drought
stories.summary:
San Miguel County is installing 21,000-gallon water tanks to improve firefighting capacity as Colorado's historic drought leaves more than 4 million residents in critically dry conditions. Sheriff Dan Covault cited worsening drought and elevated wildfire risk as the primary drivers of the project, calling it an essential resource for emergency responders and local communities.

Federal officials have indicated drought conditions may improve in some southeastern Colorado regions, but the Denver metro area and high country remain among the most severely affected zones. The Front Range and Eastern Plains have seen limited precipitation and reduced spring green-up, compounding fire risk heading into summer.

Colorado has experienced historically dry weather over the past year, and the new water reserves are intended to give rural firefighters a critical edge when responding to remote or fast-moving fires. The effort reflects broader statewide investments in wildfire preparedness as conditions across the West deteriorate.

stories.headline: Colorado Enacts First-Ever Licensing Law for Autism Therapy Providers
stories.summary: Gov. Jared Polis last week signed House Bill 1425, making Colorado the first state to require licensing for providers of applied behavior analysis, the most common therapy for children with autism. The law creates a new board to certify ABA analysts and technicians, and classifies unlicensed practice as a misdemeanor. It also empowers the state to regulate clinics treating at least three children.

stories.headline: Polis Vetoes Credit Card Swipe Fee Bill, Immigration Lawsuit Measure and Firefighter Cancer Coverage
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis has vetoed at least three bills passed by the Colorado General Assembly, including measures addressing credit card interchange fees, state participation in an immigration-related federal lawsuit and expanded cancer coverage for firefighters. The vetoes were announced in a batch, drawing attention to the governor's ongoing friction with the legislature on several policy fronts heading into the final stretch of his term.

The credit card swipe fee bill would have restricted certain interchange fees charged to merchants, a measure backed by retail interests but opposed by the financial sector. The firefighter cancer coverage bill would have expanded health benefits for first responders diagnosed with occupational cancers, an issue that has gained traction in multiple states in recent years.

Polis is term-limited and cannot seek re-election in 2026. His vetoes come as both Democratic and Republican primary candidates are competing to succeed him, with the gubernatorial primary scheduled for June 30.

stories.headline: Colorado Appeals Court Orders Retrial for Paramedics Convicted in Elijah McClain's Death
stories.summary:
A Colorado appeals court has ordered a new trial for the two paramedics convicted in connection with the 2019 death of Elijah McClain, the Aurora man who died after being injected with ketamine during a police encounter. The ruling represents a significant legal development in one of the state's most closely watched criminal justice cases stemming from the George Floyd-era reckoning over police and emergency medical conduct.

McClain, 23, died after Aurora police stopped him while he was walking home and applied a carotid hold. Paramedics Peter Cichuniec and Jeremy Cooper administered ketamine to sedate him; McClain suffered cardiac arrest and died days later. Both were convicted following a high-profile trial.

The appeals court's decision to grant a retrial could extend a legal process that has already spanned several years and generated sustained public scrutiny of Aurora's emergency services and the use of chemical sedation by first responders. The case has had broad implications for ketamine protocols used by paramedics statewide.

stories.headline: Broncos Offensive Lineman Alex Palczewski 'Ecstatic' After Signing Two-Year, $9.5 Million Contract
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos offensive lineman Alex Palczewski signed a two-year, $9.5 million contract this offseason after stepping in to start 10 games at left guard in 2025, a position he had never played before, following an injury to Ben Powers. The deal includes a $2.5 million signing bonus, $5.75 million in guarantees and incentives that could push the total value to $11 million. Palczewski, who went undrafted out of college in 2023, said the contract represented a milestone in a journey that began with a full season on injured reserve.

The Broncos finalized the deal on March 5, ahead of the club's broader free-agency signings, signaling how highly head coach Sean Payton and general manager George Paton value Palczewski's versatility along the offensive line. Palczewski said he left his exit meeting unsure of his future before receiving assurances from the coaching staff that he was part of the team's plans.

The signing comes as the Broncos prepare to move into a new $175 million training facility and headquarters, which Palczewski recalled first hearing about during his rookie year on injured reserve. The team is expected to begin using the new building within weeks, ahead of training camp next month.

stories.headline: Update: Broncos Linebacker Jonathon Cooper Issues Public Apology Following Domestic Violence Arrest
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos linebacker Jonathon Cooper has issued a public apology in the wake of his domestic violence arrest, addressing the incident for the first time publicly. The apology marks a new development in a story that drew significant attention when Cooper's arrest was first reported. The Broncos have not announced any roster or disciplinary action against Cooper in connection with the incident.

Cooper, a key contributor to Denver's defensive unit, faces potential league discipline under the NFL's personal conduct policy in addition to any legal proceedings. The situation adds uncertainty to the Broncos' linebacker corps heading into training camp.

The team has not commented in detail on Cooper's status, and the legal case remains ongoing. The apology is the first substantive public statement from Cooper since his arrest.

stories.headline: Kyle Freeland Sets Rockies Innings-Pitched Record as Brewers Complete Sweep with 12-4 Win
stories.summary:
Colorado Rockies left-hander Kyle Freeland set a franchise record for career innings pitched during Wednesday's loss to the Milwaukee Brewers, who completed a series sweep with a 12-4 victory. The milestone was a bright spot for Freeland in an otherwise difficult outing as the Rockies continued to struggle through a difficult 2026 season.

Freeland has been a cornerstone of the Rockies rotation for nearly a decade, making his longevity record a reflection of durability in a career spent largely at altitude. The Brewers' lopsided victory continued Colorado's pattern of large-margin losses during the current campaign.

The sweep adds pressure on the Rockies front office as the team searches for answers offensively and in the bullpen. Denver native Freeland's record achievement was nonetheless celebrated by the organization and Rockies fans as a meaningful individual landmark.

stories.headline: Michelin-Starred Wolf's Tailor to Launch Summer Pop-Up 'SHEEP' on Cherry Creek Rooftop
stories.summary:
Chef Kelly Whitaker, whose Wolf's Tailor holds Colorado's only two-Michelin-star rating, will open a three-month pop-up concept called SHEEP on the sixth-floor rooftop of the Clayton Hotel & Members Club in Cherry Creek beginning July 8. The concept, a play on the Wolf's Tailor name, will feature open-fire cooking and a nightly four-course menu, departing from the tasting menu format of the flagship restaurant. The rooftop venue, which seats 54, will be open to the public for the first time.

Dishes will include smoked heirloom tomato jelly with frozen wasabi, mackerel with tom kha cream, sourdough piada with bone marrow butter and caviar, and squab with sourdough gochujang. Seasonal cocktail and wine pairings will also be offered. The menu was developed by Whitaker alongside Wolf's Tailor chef Taylor Stark.

The pop-up runs through Oct. 4, 2026, with the base tasting menu priced at $95 per person before add-ons and beverages. Reservations are available through OpenTable, with walk-ins also welcome. Whitaker described SHEEP as an extension of Wolf's Tailor's values, focused on sourcing relationships and seasonal ingredients with 'greater immediacy and simplicity.'

stories.headline: Brannan Sand & Gravel Sells Colorado and Texas Operations to Vulcan Materials
stories.summary:
Denver-based Brannan Sand & Gravel has sold its Colorado and Texas operations to Vulcan Materials, one of the nation's largest producers of construction aggregates. The transaction is part of Vulcan's strategy to expand its aggregates-focused business across key growth markets in the South and Mountain West.

Brannan Sand & Gravel has been a fixture in the Denver-area construction supply market, providing sand, gravel and related materials to residential and commercial projects throughout the region. The sale represents a significant ownership change in a sector that underpins much of Colorado's ongoing construction activity.

Terms of the deal were not disclosed. The acquisition follows broader consolidation trends in the construction materials industry, where large publicly traded companies like Vulcan have been acquiring regional operators to expand geographic reach and production capacity.

stories.headline: Romance-Only Bookstore and Cafe to Open This Summer Along South Broadway in Englewood
stories.summary:
A romance-genre-only bookstore and cafe is set to open this summer on South Broadway in Englewood, adding to the corridor's growing collection of independent retail and dining destinations. The concept is among a handful of specialty independent bookstores to open in the Denver metro area in recent years, reflecting sustained consumer interest in curated retail experiences.

The shop will focus exclusively on romance novels, catering to one of the publishing industry's most consistent bestselling genres. A cafe component will allow customers to browse and read on site, following a model that has proven successful for independent booksellers nationally.

A specific opening date and address were not immediately available. South Broadway has seen a surge in small business openings in recent years, driven in part by relatively lower commercial rents compared to central Denver neighborhoods.

joke.joke: Very rarely is anything resolved in real life. That's not the way life works. Things just sort of ooze along.

topStory.headline: U.S. and Iran Exchange Strikes for Second Consecutive Night; Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz
topStory.summary:
The United States and Iran exchanged strikes for a second consecutive night into Thursday, escalating a military confrontation that began when President Donald Trump announced 'major combat operations' against Iran on Feb. 28 with joint U.S.-Israeli strikes targeting military, government and infrastructure sites. A U.S. official told ABC News that initial assessments indicated American forces intercepted nearly all missiles and drones launched by Iran on Wednesday night, with no reports of harm to U.S. personnel or major damage at U.S. bases.

U.S. Central Command confirmed its forces struck a third commercial vessel this week, firing two Hellfire missiles into the engine room of the Guinea-Bissau-flagged oil tanker M/T Jalveer in the Gulf of Oman on Wednesday after the crew allegedly failed to comply with directions from U.S. forces. The strike followed similar actions against the Palau-flagged vessels M/T Marivex and M/T Settebello earlier in the week. Three Indian sailors aboard the Settebello were killed in Tuesday's strike, a development India's shipping minister Sarbananda Sonowal described as 'deeply unfortunate,' with the ministry confirming the recovery of the sailors' bodies.

CENTCOM said its forces have disabled nine non-compliant vessels, redirected 135 ships that complied with blockade orders and allowed 42 vessels carrying humanitarian aid to pass since the blockade began on April 13. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations Centre separately confirmed a fire in the engine room of a tanker roughly 20 nautical miles northeast of Sohar, Oman, with local authorities reporting no casualties or environmental damage in connection with that incident.

The confrontation follows a failed attempt at diplomatic resolution. Initial U.S.-Iran talks held in Pakistan in April did not produce a peace deal, after which Trump announced an open-ended extension of a ceasefire along with continuation of the naval blockade until negotiations conclude 'one way or the other.' The broader conflict began with joint U.S.-Israeli strikes in late February targeting Iranian military, government and infrastructure sites.

In a significant escalation with potential global economic consequences, the Persian Gulf Strait Authority — a newly created Iranian entity established to manage shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and collect transit fees — announced on Friday that the strait would be closed 'until further notice.' The Strait of Hormuz is one of the world's most critical oil shipping chokepoints, and its closure threatens to disrupt global energy markets and international commerce.
